title: Attribute Based Interpretable Evaluation Metrics for Generative Models

reviews.summary: Paper proposes an evaluation metric for generative models which compare the distributions of real and generated images using a predefined set of attributes, or pairwise occurrences of attributes. The advantage of these metrics over the previous work is that they provide explicit visibility of which aspects contribute to the final metric value.
reviews.strengths: The idea for the proposed metric is novel – it uses language-image models to measure alignment of distributions of real and generated images given a set of text attributes. Additionally, the metric is highly customizable for downstream tasks because users may define their own set of attributes that are of interest to the specific task and drop irrelevant attributes.
reviews.weaknesses:
Failure modes of the metric are not discussed (or limitations of language-image models and how they affect the metric). 

Consider a scenario where there are two models, A and B, with the same SaKLD. Model A produces essentially perfect alignment on all attributes other than one which fails dramatically, causing a large spike in SaKLD histogram, and this attribute is the only contributing to the final score. Model B on the other hand performs poorly across all attributes but averaging over attributes yields the same SaKLD score as the model A. In this scenario SaKLD would potentially not agree with human judgment, since failing in a single attribute might not be visible when inspecting large image grids. Can this kind of scenario occur in practice, and if it can, what would be your recommendation for the user of the metric in that case?

Fig. 4 shows that SaKLD and PaKLD are dominated by few attributes of attribute pairs. Is this usually the case in practice? Fig. 5 (b) also indicates that adding new attributes contribute to the metric with diminishing strength. This might be misleading for the user of the metric. Intuitively, adding a large set of attributes should correspond to more thorough evaluation of the model, however, this might not be the case if few attributes are dominating the final value of the metric.

The empirical effectiveness of the attribute based metric is not fully demonstrated. The authors advocated for an interpretable metric but unfortunately end up comparing modern generative models using single scalar numbers (as the existing metrics do), instead of taking advantage of the interpretability of the metric and showing a more fine-grained analysis of the models.
reviews.questions:
1. Fig. 2 shows CS and DCS values for distinct attributes, how would these scores look like for pairs of “opposite attributes”, e.g., “long hair - short hair” or “makeup - no makeup”? Would DCS values be symmetric around zero or something else?

2. The description of Tab. 1 was slightly unclear, how is Tab. 1 exactly calculated? The numerical values seem to be relatively close to each other, is the difference here significant? Tab. 1 shows mean accuracy but what does the accuracy distribution looks like?

3. Automatic extractions of text attributes is an interesting idea, how robust BLIP is with various datasets? What are the most frequently extracted attributes?

Minor notes:

4. Sec. 3.3 a capital letter is missing
5. Line 154: Needs to be “the center of the images”
6. Line 220: $n$ and $P_2$ are not defined
7. Tab. 3 would benefit from also having a column for FID to see how SaKLD and PaKLD differ from it
reviews.limitations: The authors adequately addressed the limitations of their work.
reviews.rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
Paper proposes an evaluation metric for generative models which compare the distributions of real and generated images using a predefined set of attributes, or pairwise occurrences of attributes. The advantage of these metrics over the previous work is that they provide explicit visibility of which aspects contribute to the final metric value.

Strengths:
The idea for the proposed metric is novel – it uses language-image models to measure alignment of distributions of real and generated images given a set of text attributes. Additionally, the metric is highly customizable for downstream tasks because users may define their own set of attributes that are of interest to the specific task and drop irrelevant attributes.

Weaknesses:
Failure modes of the metric are not discussed (or limitations of language-image models and how they affect the metric). 

Consider a scenario where there are two models, A and B, with the same SaKLD. Model A produces essentially perfect alignment on all attributes other than one which fails dramatically, causing a large spike in SaKLD histogram, and this attribute is the only contributing to the final score. Model B on the other hand performs poorly across all attributes but averaging over attributes yields the same SaKLD score as the model A. In this scenario SaKLD would potentially not agree with human judgment, since failing in a single attribute might not be visible when inspecting large image grids. Can this kind of scenario occur in practice, and if it can, what would be your recommendation for the user of the metric in that case?

Fig. 4 shows that SaKLD and PaKLD are dominated by few attributes of attribute pairs. Is this usually the case in practice? Fig. 5 (b) also indicates that adding new attributes contribute to the metric with diminishing strength. This might be misleading for the user of the metric. Intuitively, adding a large set of attributes should correspond to more thorough evaluation of the model, however, this might not be the case if few attributes are dominating the final value of the metric.

The empirical effectiveness of the attribute based metric is not fully demonstrated. The authors advocated for an interpretable metric but unfortunately end up comparing modern generative models using single scalar numbers (as the existing metrics do), instead of taking advantage of the interpretability of the metric and showing a more fine-grained analysis of the models.

Questions:
1. Fig. 2 shows CS and DCS values for distinct attributes, how would these scores look like for pairs of “opposite attributes”, e.g., “long hair - short hair” or “makeup - no makeup”? Would DCS values be symmetric around zero or something else?

2. The description of Tab. 1 was slightly unclear, how is Tab. 1 exactly calculated? The numerical values seem to be relatively close to each other, is the difference here significant? Tab. 1 shows mean accuracy but what does the accuracy distribution looks like?

3. Automatic extractions of text attributes is an interesting idea, how robust BLIP is with various datasets? What are the most frequently extracted attributes?

Minor notes:

4. Sec. 3.3 a capital letter is missing
5. Line 154: Needs to be “the center of the images”
6. Line 220: $n$ and $P_2$ are not defined
7. Tab. 3 would benefit from also having a column for FID to see how SaKLD and PaKLD differ from it

Limitations:
The authors adequately addressed the limitations of their work.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
# Peer Review: Attribute-Based Evaluation Metrics for Generative Models

## Summary

This paper introduces a novel attribute-based evaluation framework for generative models, proposing two metrics (SaKLD and PaKLD) that leverage language-image models to assess the alignment of attribute distributions between real and generated images. The core contribution—providing interpretable metric scores by explicitly decomposing them into attribute-level contributions—addresses a genuine gap in existing evaluation methodologies. While the technical approach is sound and the underlying idea has merit, the paper falls short in demonstrating practical effectiveness and adequately addressing critical limitations of the proposed approach.

## Strengths

The paper makes several valuable contributions. First, the use of language-image models (CLIP, BLIP) to measure attribute distribution alignment is a novel and technically sound approach that enables fine-grained evaluation of generative models. Second, and importantly, the metrics provide explicit visibility into which attributes drive the final scores, offering a significant interpretability advantage over scalar-only metrics like FID or Inception Score. This transparency is valuable for practitioners seeking to understand model strengths and weaknesses.

The customizability of the metric is another strength. By allowing users to define their own attribute sets tailored to specific downstream tasks, the framework offers flexibility that generic metrics cannot provide. Additionally, the paper demonstrates solid technical execution with no major concerns regarding evaluation methodology, reproducibility, or resource requirements.

## Major Weaknesses

Despite these strengths, several critical issues limit the paper's impact. Most notably, the paper advocates for interpretability but fails to deliver on this promise. While the metrics decompose scores by attribute, the empirical analysis relies primarily on scalar comparisons rather than leveraging the fine-grained insights the metrics are designed to provide. The paper would be significantly strengthened by detailed attribute-level analysis that actually demonstrates how the interpretability advantage translates to actionable insights.

A second major concern is the dominance of few attributes in determining final metric values (evident in Fig. 4). This finding is troubling because it suggests the metrics may be misleading despite their interpretability claims. Users might be given a false sense of comprehensive evaluation when in fact only a handful of attributes drive the scores. The paper provides no guidance on how practitioners should interpret or act upon this observation.

The diminishing returns observed when adding attributes (Fig. 5b) presents another conceptual problem. The intuition that more comprehensive attribute evaluation should yield more thorough assessment is contradicted by empirical results, particularly for diffusion models where color-related attributes show saturation effects. This raises fundamental questions about what the metrics actually measure as the attribute set grows.

Furthermore, the paper inadequately addresses failure modes and limitations of the underlying language-image models. CLIP and BLIP have known biases and failure cases, yet the paper does not discuss how these limitations propagate to the proposed metrics. Without this analysis, users cannot assess the reliability of their results.

## Technical Issues

Several technical details require clarification. In Table 1, the mean accuracy values between CLIPScore and Directional CLIPScore appear quite close, but the statistical significance of observed differences is not reported. This raises questions about the practical importance of these improvements.

The behavior of DCS values for opposite attributes (e.g., "long hair" versus "short hair") is not analyzed or documented, which is a notable omission given that attribute pairs could reveal interesting model biases. Additionally, equation 6 contains undefined variables ($n$ and $P_2$) that should be clarified.

The robustness of BLIP attribute extraction across diverse datasets deserves more thorough investigation. The paper should document the distribution of extracted attributes and identify potential systematic biases in attribute extraction across different image types.

## Minor Issues and Suggestions

To strengthen the paper, I recommend the following:

1. **Discuss failure scenarios explicitly**: Provide examples where two models with identical SaKLD scores might have dramatically different attribute patterns (e.g., one catastrophically failing on a single attribute versus consistent mediocrity across all attributes). This would help users understand metric limitations.

2. **Provide practical guidance**: Offer recommendations for practitioners when few attributes dominate metric values. Should they trust these metrics? Should they expand the attribute set?

3. **Demonstrate interpretability in practice**: Move beyond scalar comparisons and provide detailed attribute-level analysis showing how the metrics reveal meaningful differences between models.

4. **Include baseline comparisons**: Add FID scores to Table 3 to enable direct comparison with established metrics and contextualize the proposed metrics' performance.

5. **Clarify statistical significance**: Report confidence intervals or statistical tests for Table 1 results to establish the significance of improvements.

6. **Analyze attribute pair behavior**: Investigate and document how the metric handles opposite attribute pairs, which could reveal important information about model biases.

## Overall Assessment

This paper presents a technically solid contribution that addresses a real need for more interpretable evaluation metrics in generative modeling. The proposed approach is novel and the execution is competent. However, the work is held back by a gap between its interpretability promise and its empirical delivery. The paper advocates for interpretable metrics but relies on scalar comparisons rather than leveraging the attribute-level insights it provides. Additionally, concerning empirical observations about attribute dominance and diminishing returns are not adequately explained or addressed.

Despite these limitations, the core idea has value and the technical foundation is sound. With revisions addressing the interpretability gap, failure mode discussion, and practical guidance for users, this work could make a meaningful contribution to the field. The customizability and transparency of the proposed metrics offer genuine advantages over existing approaches, even if the current presentation does not fully realize this potential.

## Recommendation

**Accept with Revisions** (6: Weak Accept)

The paper makes a solid technical contribution with moderate-to-high impact potential. While significant weaknesses exist in demonstrating practical effectiveness and discussing limitations, the core novelty is sound and the work is reproducible. The authors should address the major concerns outlined above, particularly regarding interpretability demonstration and failure mode analysis, to strengthen the final version.

The advantage of these metrics over the previous work is that they provide explicit visibility of which aspects contribute to the final metric value.

Strengths:
The idea for the proposed metric is novel – it uses language-image models to measure alignment of distributions of real and generated images given a set of text attributes. Additionally, the metric is highly customizable for downstream tasks because users may define their own set of attributes that are of interest to the specific task and drop irrelevant attributes.

Weaknesses:
Failure modes of the metric are not discussed (or limitations of language-image models and how they affect the metric). 

Consider a scenario where there are two models, A and B, with the same SaKLD. reviews.original_rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

reviews.summary: This paper proposes two new metrics allowing to measure and explain the diversity of a generated set of images w.r.t a training set. Instead of the usual distributional distances relying upon an embedding space from a pre-trained model, these metrics rely on a set of textual attributes. The similarity between an image and an attribute is computed using their representation in a common semantic space, via the CLIP model - vectors are shifted using a centre of training images/attributes to make similarity scores more meaningful. Several ways to obtain attributes (Captioning, User-based or GPT-based) are investigated. The usefulness of the metrics is tested with an experiment injecting images correlated with target attributes in data, an experiment aiming to detect a specific attribute relationship in a curated dataset, and in a comparison of several generative models.
reviews.strengths:
- This paper proposes an interesting approach, aiming at using the common semantic space between images and text proposed by CLIP to measure attribute and image relatedness, in order to provide interpretable representations of generated images and measure attribute-based metrics between a reference and generated dataset; such an application seems relatively original to me. 
- The two first experiments demonstrate well the usefulness of the approach, able to detect a shift when the data has been curated by human, based on which attributes.
reviews.weaknesses:
- The presentation of the paper could be improved upon. This include the writing, and the readability of the figures, as well as the quantity of information provided. This last points concerns mainly the presentation and framing of the problem of interpretability of representations, as well as the presentation and motivation of the experimental settings. 
- In particular, the paper lacks related work on concept-based representation for interpretability of images. While building metrics dedicated to generative model seems new to me, there are based on an idea which has been explored extensively before. See for example "Concept Whitening for Interpretable Image Recognition, Chen et al, 2020". 
- The paper focuses on a narrow choice of methods to generate attributes, which, to me, should be one of the key experimental investigation of the paper. Notably, the previous literature explores using different kind of attributes, coming from existing data (for example, "Interpretable Basis Decomposition for Visual Explanation, Zhou et al, 2018") or to be learnt ("A Framework to Learn with Interpretation", Parekh et al, 2021). The authors only (very shortly) argue about the number of needed attributes.
- The toy experiments seem relevant but are very fastly presented and should be expanded upon. The remaining experiments are too short to be convincing and only focus on a handful of models.
reviews.questions:
- Could you better formalize the notation $DCS_{\mathcal{X}}(a_{i,j})$ for joint distributions ?
- Is it necessary to center the text and images differently ? They lie in the same semantic space. Also, would it not be reasonable to be using the attributes themselves, rather than captions ?
reviews.limitations: - Previous distributional metrics are several times referred as relying on external models in your paper. However, the attributes that you use also rely on external models (except the USER one, of course - but in this case, the computing of DCS still relies on a captioning model). How would you address this issue ?

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes two new metrics allowing to measure and explain the diversity of a generated set of images w.r.t a training set. Instead of the usual distributional distances relying upon an embedding space from a pre-trained model, these metrics rely on a set of textual attributes. The similarity between an image and an attribute is computed using their representation in a common semantic space, via the CLIP model - vectors are shifted using a centre of training images/attributes to make similarity scores more meaningful. Several ways to obtain attributes (Captioning, User-based or GPT-based) are investigated. The usefulness of the metrics is tested with an experiment injecting images correlated with target attributes in data, an experiment aiming to detect a specific attribute relationship in a curated dataset, and in a comparison of several generative models.

Strengths:
- This paper proposes an interesting approach, aiming at using the common semantic space between images and text proposed by CLIP to measure attribute and image relatedness, in order to provide interpretable representations of generated images and measure attribute-based metrics between a reference and generated dataset; such an application seems relatively original to me. 
- The two first experiments demonstrate well the usefulness of the approach, able to detect a shift when the data has been curated by human, based on which attributes.

Weaknesses:
- The presentation of the paper could be improved upon. This include the writing, and the readability of the figures, as well as the quantity of information provided. This last points concerns mainly the presentation and framing of the problem of interpretability of representations, as well as the presentation and motivation of the experimental settings. 
- In particular, the paper lacks related work on concept-based representation for interpretability of images. While building metrics dedicated to generative model seems new to me, there are based on an idea which has been explored extensively before. See for example "Concept Whitening for Interpretable Image Recognition, Chen et al, 2020". 
- The paper focuses on a narrow choice of methods to generate attributes, which, to me, should be one of the key experimental investigation of the paper. Notably, the previous literature explores using different kind of attributes, coming from existing data (for example, "Interpretable Basis Decomposition for Visual Explanation, Zhou et al, 2018") or to be learnt ("A Framework to Learn with Interpretation", Parekh et al, 2021). The authors only (very shortly) argue about the number of needed attributes.
- The toy experiments seem relevant but are very fastly presented and should be expanded upon. The remaining experiments are too short to be convincing and only focus on a handful of models.

Questions:
- Could you better formalize the notation $DCS_{\mathcal{X}}(a_{i,j})$ for joint distributions ?
- Is it necessary to center the text and images differently ? They lie in the same semantic space. Also, would it not be reasonable to be using the attributes themselves, rather than captions ?

Limitations:
- Previous distributional metrics are several times referred as relying on external models in your paper. However, the attributes that you use also rely on external models (except the USER one, of course - but in this case, the computing of DCS still relies on a captioning model). How would you address this issue ?

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
# Peer Review: Interpretable Evaluation Metrics for Generative Models Using CLIP-Based Attributes

## Summary

This paper presents an interpretable framework for evaluating generative models by leveraging CLIP's semantic space and textual attributes. The authors introduce Directional CLIPScore (DCS) and propose two metrics—Single attribute KL Divergence (SaKLD) and Paired attribute KL divergence (PaKLD)—to measure distributional differences between training and generated datasets. While the core idea is interesting and the technical approach is sound, the paper would benefit from significant improvements in presentation and more comprehensive experimental validation.

## Strengths

The paper makes a solid contribution to an important problem: achieving interpretability in generative model evaluation. The use of CLIP's semantic space to establish attribute-image relationships is a relatively novel approach that provides meaningful interpretations of model behavior. The technical foundation is appropriate, with well-motivated use of centered embeddings and kernel density estimation for probability estimation.

The first two experiments effectively demonstrate the practical utility of the approach. The toy experiments successfully show how the metrics can detect attribute shifts and identify differences between curated and uncurated datasets, which validates the core premise. Additionally, the proposed metrics offer genuine interpretability advantages by grounding evaluations in human-understandable attributes rather than abstract embedding spaces.

## Major Weaknesses

**Presentation Quality**: The paper suffers from presentation issues that hinder accessibility. The writing could be more polished, figures need improved readability, and key concepts are not always clearly explained. These issues make it difficult for readers to fully engage with the technical contributions.

**Incomplete Related Work**: The paper inadequately addresses existing literature on concept-based interpretability methods. Most notably, the work by Chen et al. (2020) on Concept Whitening for Interpretable Image Recognition and other related approaches in this space are not discussed. This omission weakens the positioning of the contribution and suggests incomplete awareness of the broader context.

**Methodological Contradiction**: A significant concern is the apparent inconsistency in the paper's argument. The authors criticize existing metrics for relying on external models (like CLIP), yet their approach similarly depends on CLIP for embeddings and BLIP for attribute extraction. This reliance is not adequately justified or addressed, creating a logical gap in the motivation.

**Limited Experimental Scope**: The experimental validation is insufficient. The toy experiments are presented too briefly without adequate analysis, and the remaining experiments evaluate only a handful of models with insufficient depth. To be convincing, the paper needs evaluation across a broader range of generative models and more detailed analysis of each experiment.

**Insufficient Attribute Investigation**: Despite attribute selection being a critical component of the approach, this aspect receives minimal experimental attention. The paper only briefly discusses the number of required attributes and explores three selection methods without thorough comparison or analysis. Given the importance of attribute quality to the overall framework, this deserves substantially more investigation.

## Minor Issues

**Notation and Formalization**: The notation for joint distributions, specifically DCS_X(a_{i,j}), requires better formalization to ensure clarity and reproducibility.

**Centering Strategy**: The paper should clarify whether separate centering of text and image embeddings is necessary given that both exist in the same CLIP semantic space. Additionally, exploring whether attributes can be used directly rather than captions for centering would strengthen the approach.

**Attribute Sources**: The investigation of alternative attribute sources from existing literature—such as data-driven approaches or learned attributes—would provide valuable insights into the robustness of the framework.

## Suggestions for Improvement

1. Substantially expand and more thoroughly present the toy experiments with detailed analysis and clearer exposition
2. Conduct comprehensive experiments evaluating diverse generative models to strengthen empirical validation
3. Provide rigorous investigation of attribute selection methods as a primary experimental focus
4. Improve overall presentation quality, including writing clarity and figure design
5. Add discussion of related work on concept-based interpretability methods
6. Address the apparent contradiction regarding external model reliance
7. Better formalize mathematical notation, particularly for joint distributions
8. Explore alternative attribute sources and centering strategies

## Overall Assessment

This paper addresses an important problem with a technically sound approach that offers genuine interpretability advantages for generative model evaluation. The core contribution is solid and the initial experiments show promise. However, the work is held back by presentation issues, incomplete related work discussion, limited experimental scope, and insufficient investigation of key methodological choices. The experimental validation needs to be substantially expanded to be truly convincing, and the attribute selection methodology deserves more rigorous treatment given its centrality to the approach.

With revisions addressing these concerns—particularly expanding the experiments, improving presentation, and better contextualizing the work within existing literature—this could become a strong contribution to the field. In its current form, however, the paper represents a promising but incomplete effort that requires significant development before publication.

## Recommendation

**Weak Accept**: The paper presents technically solid work with moderate-to-high potential impact on generative model evaluation. The core ideas are sound and the approach is novel enough to merit publication. However, the presentation needs improvement, the experimental validation requires expansion, and several methodological aspects need better justification. These are issues that can be addressed through revision rather than fundamental flaws with the contribution itself. The work would benefit significantly from the suggested improvements, but the underlying contribution is sufficient to warrant acceptance with the expectation of addressing these concerns.

reviews.summary: The embedding space used for calculating FID is computed with Inception V3 which is trained for image classification as the target task. This means it is more likely to capture discriminative features, raising doubts about its ability to effectively evaluate generative models. There is also a need to devise a new evaluation metric that can interpret underlying factors. This paper introduces a method called Directional CLIP Score (DCS) to properly evaluate this. The pre-trained CLIP is used as the embedding space. In particular, the paper proposes "Single attribute KL div (SaKLD)" to measure single attribute alignment and "Paired attribute KL div (PaKLD)" to measure multiple attribute alignment as new metrics. This paper provides some insightful measurement result using the proposed metrics.
reviews.strengths: The effort to evaluate generative models from various perspectives through the introduction of such metrics can be considered novel and a contribution. Especially considering the current issues such as the bias in stable diffusion [1], the proposal of such metrics can bring benefits to the field from the perspective of trustworthy AI.
reviews.weaknesses:
It appears to be an intrinsic limitation that a significant number of samples (50k) are still required to obtain stable results. Nonetheless, thanks to the reported findings, we can gain more insights, and I appreciate that. 
Remaining concerns are written in [Questions] section.
reviews.questions:
1) In lines 96-97, it seems that the authors of the referenced paper already propose using the CLIP embedding space for evaluating generative model? If not, it would be helpful to clarify the differences compared to previously proposed methods. Regarding the for explainable evaluation in the related work section, it is necessary to determine whether this paper is the first to come up with it. If there are any additional previous works, it would be helpful to share the, 

2) The major concern raised is the reliance on the embedding space. What if there are biases in the CLIP space itself? For example, if being female and wearing makeup are highly correlated, even a woman who does not actually wear makeup may show high similarity to makeup attribute. In such cases, it becomes difficult to consider CLIP score or CLIP direction score as accurate measures of similarity. While a more disentangled multimodal latent space may help alleviate this problem, I'm curious about the authors' perspective on this issue.

3) Directional CLIP Score adopts a method of computing the center of training images and normalizing them. Does this mean that anyone who wants to evaluate a generative model needs to share all these training images? Also, considering the introduction of the auxiliary image captioning model called BLIP, the improvement in performance seems minimal. Do the authors believe it is worth the trade-off?

4) Question about Table 5: cI may have missed it, but was there an explanation for the last column of FID_clip in Table 5? What does it represent? Also, in Table 5, it seems that SaKLD is calculated for a single attribute. How is it computed?

5) Can PaKLD be proposed for more than two attributes? It would be great if judgments could be made for a wide range of attributes as shown in Figure 1 (b).

6) How should we interpret numerical values itself of the scores?  Although it may not be as interpretable as accuracy I'm still curious about the authors' insights based on their experience.
reviews.limitations: It seems that there are no particular specific limitations.

The embedding space used for calculating FID is computed with Inception V3 which is trained for image classification as the target task. This means it is more likely to capture discriminative features, raising doubts about its ability to effectively evaluate generative models. There is also a need to devise a new evaluation metric that can interpret underlying factors. This paper introduces a method called Directional CLIP Score (DCS) to properly evaluate this. The pre-trained CLIP is used as the embedding space. In particular, the paper proposes "Single attribute KL div (SaKLD)" to measure single attribute alignment and "Paired attribute KL div (PaKLD)" to measure multiple attribute alignment as new metrics. This paper provides some insightful measurement result using the proposed metrics.

Strengths:
The effort to evaluate generative models from various perspectives through the introduction of such metrics can be considered novel and a contribution. Especially considering the current issues such as the bias in stable diffusion [1], the proposal of such metrics can bring benefits to the field from the perspective of trustworthy AI.

Weaknesses:
It appears to be an intrinsic limitation that a significant number of samples (50k) are still required to obtain stable results. Nonetheless, thanks to the reported findings, we can gain more insights, and I appreciate that. 
Remaining concerns are written in [Questions] section.

2) The major concern raised is the reliance on the embedding space. What if there are biases in the CLIP space itself? For example, if being female and wearing makeup are highly correlated, even a woman who does not actually wear makeup may show high similarity to makeup attribute. In such cases, it becomes difficult to consider CLIP score or CLIP direction score as accurate measures of similarity. While a more disentangled multimodal latent space may help alleviate this problem, I'm curious about the authors' perspective on this issue.

Also, considering the introduction of the auxiliary image captioning model called BLIP, the improvement in performance seems minimal. Do the authors believe it is worth the trade-off?

reviews.text:
# Peer Review: Interpretable Evaluation Metrics for Generative Models

## Summary

This paper proposes a novel approach to evaluating generative models through interpretable, attribute-based metrics using CLIP embedding space. The authors introduce Directional CLIP Score (DCS) and related metrics (SaKLD and PaKLD) to address limitations of existing metrics like FID, which rely on embedding spaces designed for classification rather than generation tasks. While the work makes a meaningful contribution to trustworthy AI and interpretable evaluation, several concerns regarding clarity, practical limitations, and potential biases require attention.

## Strengths

The paper tackles a genuine problem in the field: the mismatch between the design objectives of standard evaluation metrics and their application to generative model assessment. The observation that FID's reliance on Inception V3 embeddings—trained for classification—represents a fundamental limitation is well-founded and motivates the proposed direction.

The authors' effort to develop interpretable, multi-perspective evaluation metrics is commendable and represents a valuable contribution to trustworthy AI. By proposing attribute-based metrics, the work enables practitioners to understand not just whether a model performs well, but *how* it performs across specific dimensions. The paper's application to bias detection in generative models (e.g., Stable Diffusion) demonstrates practical relevance.

The empirical results presented using the proposed metrics provide insightful analysis of model behavior, suggesting that the framework can yield meaningful insights about generative model performance.

## Weaknesses and Concerns

Despite these strengths, several significant limitations warrant discussion.

**Sample Size Requirements and Practical Applicability**: The requirement of 50,000 samples to obtain stable results represents a substantial computational burden. While this may be an intrinsic limitation of the approach, it raises questions about practical applicability, particularly for researchers with limited computational resources. The paper would benefit from discussing whether this requirement can be reduced or whether alternative strategies exist.

**Unaddressed Bias in CLIP Space**: The paper relies heavily on CLIP embeddings yet does not adequately address potential biases inherent in this embedding space itself. The authors acknowledge that attributes may correlate (e.g., female and makeup) but do not thoroughly investigate how such correlations might undermine the reliability of their metrics. Exploring more disentangled multimodal latent spaces could help alleviate these concerns.

**Novelty and Clarity**: The paper references prior work using CLIP embeddings for evaluation (lines 96-97) but fails to clearly articulate how this work differs from those approaches. It remains unclear whether this is the first work proposing explainable evaluation metrics using CLIP space, or whether the novelty lies elsewhere. This distinction must be clarified.

**Practical Limitations of DCS**: The requirement to maintain and share training image sets to compute the center of training data (CX) for normalization may limit the method's applicability, particularly for proprietary models or large-scale datasets.

**Limited Scope of Pairwise Analysis**: PaKLD is restricted to analyzing pairwise attribute relationships and cannot be extended to evaluate interactions among multiple attributes simultaneously. This limitation restricts the comprehensiveness of the attribute-based evaluation.

**Insufficient Explanation of Results**: Table 5 lacks clarity regarding the FID_clip column and the exact computation method for SaKLD. Additionally, the paper provides minimal guidance on interpreting absolute numerical values of the proposed scores, which limits their practical utility.

**Marginal Improvements from BLIP**: The use of the BLIP image captioning model as an auxiliary component yields only minimal performance improvements, raising questions about whether the added complexity is justified.

## Suggestions for Improvement

1. Provide a clear delineation of novelty compared to prior work using CLIP embeddings for evaluation, with explicit citations and discussion of differences.

2. Conduct a thorough investigation of potential biases in CLIP space, including analysis of attribute correlations and their impact on metric reliability.

3. Explore alternative, more disentangled embedding spaces that might reduce inherent biases while maintaining interpretability.

4. Extend PaKLD to support evaluation of relationships among more than two attributes.

5. Provide explicit guidance for practitioners on interpreting the numerical values produced by DCS, SaKLD, and PaKLD.

6. Clarify the computation methods and results presented in Table 5, particularly regarding FID_clip and SaKLD calculations.

7. Discuss strategies for reducing the 50,000-sample requirement or provide guidance on sample size selection for different use cases.

## Minor Comments

The paper would benefit from more discussion of the practical implications of requiring training image sets for DCS normalization, particularly for scenarios where such data cannot be shared.

## Overall Assessment

This paper presents a technically sound contribution to an important problem: developing interpretable evaluation metrics for generative models that account for limitations in existing approaches. The work demonstrates moderate-to-high impact potential through its focus on trustworthy AI and attribute-based evaluation. However, several concerns regarding clarity of novelty, unaddressed biases in the underlying embedding space, and practical limitations prevent this from being a strong accept. With revisions addressing the suggested points—particularly clarification of novelty, investigation of CLIP space biases, and improved exposition of results—this work would represent a solid contribution to the field.

**Recommendation**: Weak Accept

reviews.summary: This is a very interesting research work. The main contribution of this paper is to consider the attribute information in the original training data when evaluating the quality of images generated by the model. There are two benefits to this approach: 1) determining whether the model can correctly imitate the distribution of the training data; 2) explaining which attributes the model does not perform well on. In implementing this idea, the authors found that directly calculating the CLIPScore between the image representation and the text representation of the attribute does not yield distinctive results. Therefore, they proposed Directional CLIPScore (DSC). The main idea of this approach is to move the reference point for calculating vector similarity to a more reasonable point. At the same time, they proposed two methods to apply DSC, one is Single attribute KL Divergence, and the other is Paired attribute KL Divergence (PaKLD) considering the combination of attributes.

However, one method of extracting attributes is to generate an attribute list with GPT and then filter it with training data. I think the attribute list generated by GPT in advance may bring biases. The core of this paper is mainly to study the correlation between the model and the training data. reviews.text:
# Peer Review: Attribute-Aware Evaluation of Generative Models

## Summary

This paper proposes a novel evaluation framework for generative models that leverages attribute information extracted from training data. The core contribution is the Directional CLIPScore (DCS) method, which aims to improve the discriminability of similarity-based metrics by adjusting the reference point for calculation. Additionally, the authors introduce SaKLD and PaKLD metrics to measure single and paired attribute distributions respectively. While the research addresses an important and meaningful problem—making generative model evaluation more interpretable and attribute-aware—the experimental validation falls short of providing convincing quantitative evidence that the proposed metrics offer substantial improvements over existing approaches.

## Strengths

1. **Well-motivated research question**: The paper tackles an important problem in generative model evaluation. Using attribute information from training data to both assess generation quality and explain model performance gaps is a valuable and timely contribution.

2. **Elegant technical approach**: The Directional CLIPScore is concise and conceptually appealing. The solution to the low discriminability problem in raw CLIPScore by shifting the reference point for similarity calculation is intuitive and appears effective in the presented case studies.

3. **Dual utility of the framework**: The proposed approach provides two complementary benefits: (1) determining whether models correctly imitate the training data distribution, and (2) identifying which specific attributes models perform poorly on, thereby offering interpretability alongside evaluation metrics.

4. **Multiple attribute extraction strategies**: The paper presents three attribute selection methods (BLIP extraction, user annotation, and GPT-based generation), offering flexibility in practical application.

## Weaknesses

1. **Potential bias in attribute extraction**: The reliance on GPT-based attribute generation raises concerns about representativeness. Since the paper's core premise is measuring correlation between models and training data distributions, biases introduced by GPT in selecting which attributes to evaluate could fundamentally compromise the validity of results. The authors should address whether the attribute list is truly representative of the training data's key characteristics.

2. **Insufficient quantitative validation**: The experimental sections (5.1-5.3) primarily verify consistency with expected outcomes rather than demonstrating clear superiority over existing metrics. While case studies provide intuitive support, the paper lacks rigorous quantitative comparisons. Specifically, there is no evidence presented that DCS, SaKLD, and PaKLD actually outperform previous evaluation metrics in terms of meaningful performance indicators.

3. **Missing human evaluation correlation analysis**: A critical gap is the absence of correlation analysis between the proposed metrics and human judgments. The paper does not report correlation coefficients between the proposed evaluation metrics and human evaluation scores, nor does it compare whether these metrics show improved correlation with human judgment compared to established baselines. This is essential for validating the practical utility of the approach.

## Technical Issues

1. **Notation inconsistency**: The variable N is overloaded with different meanings across equations. In Eq. (2), N represents the number of samples, while in Eq. (5), it denotes the number of attributes. This creates unnecessary confusion and should be resolved with clearer notation.

2. **Incomplete justification of DCS necessity**: The paper does not adequately address whether simply using Euclidean distance between E(x) and E(a) could directly resolve the discriminability issues in CLIPScore without the added complexity of the proposed DCS approach. This alternative should be discussed and empirically compared.

## Suggestions for Improvement

1. **Conduct comprehensive human evaluation correlation studies**: Perform systematic correlation analysis between the proposed metrics and human evaluation judgments. Report Pearson/Spearman correlation coefficients and compare these against correlations achieved by existing evaluation metrics.

2. **Provide quantitative superiority evidence**: Include direct quantitative comparisons demonstrating that the proposed metrics achieve higher correlation with human judgments or other established evaluation benchmarks compared to prior work.

3. **Address attribute bias concerns**: Analyze the representativeness of GPT-generated attributes and explore the sensitivity of results to attribute selection. Consider ablation studies or alternative attribute extraction methods to validate robustness.

4. **Clarify technical details**: Resolve notation inconsistencies and provide thorough justification for design choices, particularly regarding why DCS is necessary compared to simpler alternatives.

## Overall Assessment

This paper presents technically sound work on an important problem with a novel and intuitive solution. The Directional CLIPScore and associated metrics represent a meaningful contribution to interpretable generative model evaluation. However, the experimental validation is the limiting factor. The work demonstrates consistency with expected outcomes but fails to provide convincing quantitative evidence that the proposed approach outperforms existing evaluation metrics. The absence of human evaluation correlation analysis is particularly problematic for a paper proposing new evaluation metrics.

Despite these limitations, the core ideas are valuable and the technical approach is solid. With additional experimental validation—particularly correlation analysis with human judgments and comparative studies against existing metrics—this work would make a strong contribution. As it stands, the paper presents promising preliminary results that warrant publication, though with the understanding that the practical benefits of the proposed metrics remain to be fully demonstrated.

**Recommendation: Borderline Accept** – The technical soundness and novelty of the approach outweigh the limited experimental validation, but significant additional empirical work is needed to fully support the claims of metric superiority.

Summary:
This is a very interesting research work. The main contribution of this paper is to consider the attribute information in the original training data when evaluating the quality of images generated by the model. There are two benefits to this approach: 1) determining whether the model can correctly imitate the distribution of the training data; 2) explaining which attributes the model does not perform well on. In implementing this idea, the authors found that directly calculating the CLIPScore between the image representation and the text representation of the attribute does not yield distinctive results. Therefore, they proposed Directional CLIPScore (DSC). The main idea of this approach is to move the reference point for calculating vector similarity to a more reasonable point. At the same time, they proposed two methods to apply DSC, one is Single attribute KL Divergence, and the other is Paired attribute KL Divergence (PaKLD) considering the combination of attributes.

Strengths:
1. I think this research question is very interesting. It is very meaningful to use the attribute information of the dataset to help evaluate the quality of the model's generation.
2. The Directional CLIPScore (DSC) proposed by the authors is very concise and appears to be quite effective in the case study.

Weaknesses:
1. In Section 3.3, I agree with the extraction of attributes using the BLIP and manual annotation methods. However, one method of extracting attributes is to generate an attribute list with GPT and then filter it with training data. I think the attribute list generated by GPT in advance may bring biases. The core of this paper is mainly to study the correlation between the model and the training data. However, if the list generated by GPT is not the most representative attribute, the results may be biased.
2. The experimental part in Sections 5.1-5.3 does not seem very convincing. The authors mainly verify that the proposed method can indeed be consistent with some expected experimental designs, but there is a lack of more convincing quantitative indicators to show that their proposed evaluation metrics are better than those proposed by others previous research works. I would prefer to see the authors analyze the correlation coefficient between their proposed evaluation metrics and human evaluation, as well as whether their evaluation metrics have improved in terms of correlation coefficient compared to previous evaluation indicators. This is my biggest concern for this work.

Questions:
1. In Section 3.1, can the problem exhibited by CLIPScore be solved by directly calculating the Euclidean distance between E(x) and E(a) without relying on Directional CLIPScore?
2. Notation overload. The meanings of N used in Eq. (5) and Eq. (2) are different.

Limitations:
see weakness

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.

reviews.summary:
This paper proposes a new metric to evaluate the quality and diversity of generated images based on
interpretable embeddings. To obtain the interpretable embeddings for selected attributes, the cluster
centers of the encodings of two separate encoders, one for images and the other for the text attributes
are calculated and the interpretable embeddings are the difference between the encodings of each
image and attribute and their respective cluster centers. The interpretable embeddings represent the
direction in which a particular embedding lies with respect to it’s cluster center. The CLIPScore
between the interpretable embeddings of an image and an attribute is calculated and is named as
’Directional CLIPScore’, since the interpretable embeddings represent the ’direction’.
The authors have proposed two metrics : 1. SaKLD - to quatify how closely the attribute distri-
bution in generated images matches with that of training images. 2. PaKLD - to quantify correlations
between different attributes. The KL divergence between the probability density functions of the
Directional CLIPScores for all the images in the train data and generated data.
Using SaKLD, the KL divergence between the attribute distribution in training and generated
images is calculated. PaKLD calculates the KL divergence similar to SaKLD, except that the presence
of a pair of attributes is required in the training and generated images.
reviews.strengths: The motivation for the idea is good and has a huge potential impact for improving evaluation of generative models.
reviews.weaknesses:
- Although, the motivation for developing this metric is valid, the overall methodology and the
experimental results are not convincing for the use of this metric. Also, the experiments performed
are inadequate and do not sufficiently justify how well the metric performs compared to the previously
proposed metrics. Additionally, previous metrics can directly evaluate quality of generation based
on the generated images alone, but this metric heavily relies on the attributes in the form of text
descriptions. Thus, it limits the applicability and generalizability of this metric.
- The proposed metrics use CLIPScore (which already exists) for interpretable embeddings and then
applies KL Divergence for the PDFs of the ClipScores of images and attributes, thus, showing limited
novelty.
- Most of the paper is easy to follow but some important parts like, how is the center of text attributes
calculated, results from table 1 (what does accuracy stand for) etc. are a bit ambiguous. There is a
lot of scope to improve the technical soundness of the paper. Although some of the popular metrics
1are mentioned, there has been a lot of work in the generative modelling domain which the literature
survey must cover. The proposed methodology is not very sound and is not well supported with the
experimental setup. The results are also not sufficiently explained. Diversity is the main motivation
for the paper as it is mentioned in the abstract but any theoretical or empirical work to support it is
completely missing.
- Motivation and methodology including the steps to evaluate generated samples is understandable but
some parts are ambiguous (please refer above comments).
- Based on the current state of the experiments, the contributions don’t seem to be significant as there
is not enough validation to support the claims.

 typos:
- Line 43 : Instead of ”Figure 1 (b)”, it should be Figure 1 (a)
- Line 168 : Section 3.3 First letters of all the words in the heading must be capital
Other remarks :
- Line 56 : Instead of ”If the model lacks essential attributes” the following sounds technically
correct ”if model lacks ”representation” of essential attributes”.
- Line 56-58 : This claim does not seem to be correct.
- Line 147 : Link to the specified figure is missing
- Figure 2 is never refered to in the text, is it an unnecessary figure?
- Line 253 : Generated set has non-smiling men and non-smiling women. But Figure 4 caption
says otherwise
reviews.questions:
1. If I understood correctly, the text and the image encoders are different? If yes, then
the embedding space of both models is going to be very different and will be influenced by the
downstream tasks that they are trained on. Would this metric always work for embeddings from
different models? Does it make sense to calculate similarity between embeddings from different
models?
2. Table 1 represents accuracy results for CS vs DCS. What is meant by evaluating ”positive
samples” in line 164 and 165? And what is meant by ”how well they are aligned”? Also, what
exactly is the accuracy calculated for?
3. In Section 3.1, there is a mention of ”each channel of the embedding being utilized”, how exactly
does this happen?
4. Line 196-197 claims that the generated distribution is desirable only if it has exactly same number
of samples as in the training set that has a particular attribute. Why should it be? Afaik, the
underlying distribution of latents represent semantic features of the data and the generative
model is responsible to generate novel samples that could be realistic but not necessarily show
exactly same features same number of times in the training set. So, is the assumption in the
mentioned lines appropriate? Can you prove this or maybe refer to some papers that provide
such guarantees?
reviews.limitations: limitations have been addressed
reviews.rating: 4: Borderline reject: Technically solid paper where reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation, outweigh reasons to accept, e.g., good evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.confidence: 2: You are willing to defend your assessment, but it is quite likely that you did not understand the central parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.

reviews.text:
# Peer Review: Interpretable Evaluation Metrics for Generative Models Using Attribute-Based Embeddings

## Summary

This paper proposes a family of interpretable evaluation metrics for generative models based on attribute-based embeddings derived from CLIP. The authors introduce Directional CLIPScore (DCS), SaKLD (Single-attribute KL Divergence), and PaKLD (Pairwise-attribute KL Divergence) to enable explainable evaluation of generated images through predefined text attributes. While the motivation for interpretable evaluation is compelling, the paper suffers from limited novelty, inadequate experimental validation, and several technical ambiguities that undermine its contributions.

## Strengths

The paper addresses an important problem: the need for interpretable evaluation metrics in generative modeling. Current metrics often lack explainability, making it difficult to understand why models succeed or fail. The authors' motivation to leverage attribute-based embeddings for interpretability is well-founded and could have meaningful impact on the field.

The overall presentation is generally clear and easy to follow, which aids readability. The core concept of using CLIP embeddings with predefined attributes to provide attribute-level insights into model performance is intuitive and well-motivated.

## Major Weaknesses

**Limited Novelty**: The proposed approach primarily combines existing techniques—specifically, CLIPScore and KL Divergence applied to probability density functions of CLIPScores. While the combination is reasonable, the technical novelty is insufficient for a strong contribution. The paper essentially applies standard statistical methods to an existing metric without introducing fundamentally new insights or techniques.

**Inadequate Experimental Validation**: The experimental evaluation does not sufficiently justify the effectiveness of the proposed metrics compared to existing alternatives. The comparison against baseline metrics is limited, and the results do not convincingly demonstrate that the proposed approach offers meaningful advantages. Table 1 presents results (DCS mean accuracy of 0.530 vs CLIPScore's 0.409), but without clarity on what "accuracy" represents or how "positive samples" and alignment criteria are defined, these results are difficult to interpret and validate.

**Questionable Core Assumption**: The fundamental assumption that generated images should have exactly the same attribute distribution as the training set is neither theoretically justified nor empirically validated. This is a critical assumption that underpins the entire methodology, yet the paper provides no theoretical guarantees or references supporting why this should hold. Generated models may intentionally or unintentionally produce different distributions, and this should not necessarily be penalized.

**Dependence on Predefined Attributes**: The heavy reliance on text attribute descriptions significantly limits the applicability and generalizability of the proposed metrics. Unlike metrics that evaluate quality based solely on generated images, this approach requires manual specification of attributes through BLIP extraction, user annotation, or GPT-3 generation. This dependency makes the metric less flexible and potentially introduces subjectivity into the evaluation process.

**Embedding Space Mismatch**: Using embeddings from CLIP's image encoder and text encoder, which operate in different embedding spaces influenced by different downstream training objectives, may not be appropriate for direct similarity calculations. The paper does not address whether these embeddings are sufficiently aligned for the proposed comparisons.

## Technical Issues and Ambiguities

Several technical aspects require clarification:

1. **Attribute Center Calculation**: The paper does not clearly explain how the center of text attributes (CT) is computed and how these centers are utilized in the methodology. Is CT computed as a mean embedding of all attribute descriptions, or is there a more sophisticated approach?

2. **Accuracy Definition**: Table 1's accuracy metric is poorly defined. What constitutes "positive samples"? What are the alignment criteria? Without this clarity, the experimental results cannot be properly evaluated.

3. **Embedding Channel Utilization**: Section 3.1 does not adequately explain how each channel of the embeddings is utilized in the proposed methodology.

4. **Diversity Claims**: The abstract emphasizes diversity as a main motivation, yet the paper provides neither theoretical justification nor empirical evidence supporting how the proposed metrics specifically address diversity concerns.

## Minor Issues

- The literature survey is incomplete and omits significant related work in generative modeling and evaluation metrics.
- Figure 4 has a discrepancy between its caption and the corresponding text description.
- The methodology is not well-supported by the experimental setup, and results lack sufficient explanation.

## Suggestions for Improvement

1. Provide rigorous theoretical justification or comprehensive empirical evidence for the assumption that generated attribute distributions should match training set distributions exactly.
2. Clarify all technical details: how attribute centers are calculated, what accuracy in Table 1 represents, and how embedding channels are utilized.
3. Expand the literature review to adequately cover related work in generative modeling and evaluation metrics.
4. Conduct more comprehensive experimental validation comparing the proposed metrics against a broader set of existing metrics across multiple datasets.
5. Address the embedding space mismatch issue and justify why CLIP's cross-modal embeddings are appropriate for this task.
6. Provide references supporting claims about model behavior and feature distribution generation.
7. Either strengthen the diversity claims with theoretical or empirical support, or remove them from the abstract.

## Overall Assessment

While the paper tackles an important problem and the motivation for interpretable metrics is sound, the execution falls short of publication standards. The limited novelty, reliance on existing techniques without significant innovation, and inadequate experimental validation are substantial concerns. The core assumptions lack justification, technical details remain ambiguous, and the experimental results do not convincingly demonstrate the utility of the proposed approach. The restriction to predefined attributes further limits the generalizability of the work.

The paper would benefit from addressing the fundamental assumptions, providing clearer technical exposition, and conducting substantially more rigorous experimental validation. In its current form, the contributions do not appear sufficiently significant or well-validated for acceptance.

**Recommendation: Borderline Reject**

The paper presents a reasonable idea but requires substantial revisions to address the technical ambiguities, provide adequate experimental validation, and justify its core assumptions before it can make a convincing contribution to the field.

1are mentioned, there has been a lot of work in the generative modelling domain which the literature
survey must cover. The proposed methodology is not very sound and is not well supported with the
experimental setup. The results are also not sufficiently explained. Diversity is the main motivation
for the paper as it is mentioned in the abstract but any theoretical or empirical work to support it is
completely missing.
- Motivation and methodology including the steps to evaluate generated samples is understandable but
some parts are ambiguous (please refer above comments).
- Based on the current state of the experiments, the contributions don’t seem to be significant as there
is not enough validation to support the claims.

 typos:
- Line 43 : Instead of ”Figure 1 (b)”, it should be Figure 1 (a)
- Line 168 : Section 3.3 First letters of all the words in the heading must be capital
Other remarks :
- Line 56 : Instead of ”If the model lacks essential attributes” the following sounds technically
correct ”if model lacks ”representation” of essential attributes”.
- Line 56-58 : This claim does not seem to be correct.
- Line 147 : Link to the specified figure is missing
- Figure 2 is never refered to in the text, is it an unnecessary figure?
- Line 253 : Generated set has non-smiling men and non-smiling women. But Figure 4 caption
says otherwise

Questions:
1. If I understood correctly, the text and the image encoders are different? If yes, then
the embedding space of both models is going to be very different and will be influenced by the
downstream tasks that they are trained on. Would this metric always work for embeddings from
different models? Does it make sense to calculate similarity between embeddings from different
models?
2. Table 1 represents accuracy results for CS vs DCS. What is meant by evaluating ”positive
samples” in line 164 and 165? And what is meant by ”how well they are aligned”? Also, what
exactly is the accuracy calculated for?
3. In Section 3.1, there is a mention of ”each channel of the embedding being utilized”, how exactly
does this happen?
4. Line 196-197 claims that the generated distribution is desirable only if it has exactly same number
of samples as in the training set that has a particular attribute. Why should it be? Afaik, the
underlying distribution of latents represent semantic features of the data and the generative
model is responsible to generate novel samples that could be realistic but not necessarily show
exactly same features same number of times in the training set. So, is the assumption in the
mentioned lines appropriate? Can you prove this or maybe refer to some papers that provide
such guarantees?

Limitations:
limitations have been addressed

Rating:
4: Borderline reject: Technically solid paper where reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation, outweigh reasons to accept, e.g., good evaluation. Please use sparingly.

strengths: Good motivation for developing interpretable evaluation metrics with significant potential impact for improving evaluation of generative models
Overall concept of using attribute-based embeddings to provide explainability is well-motivated
Most of the paper is easy to follow
weaknesses: Limited novelty as the approach uses existing CLIPScore and applies KL Divergence to PDFs of CLIPScores
Methodology and experimental results are not convincing for the usefulness of the metric
Experiments are inadequate and do not sufficiently justify how well the metric performs compared to previously proposed metrics
Heavy reliance on text attribute descriptions limits applicability and generalizability compared to metrics that evaluate quality based on generated images alone
Important technical parts are ambiguous, including how the center of text attributes is calculated and what accuracy in Table 1 represents
Literature survey is incomplete and does not cover significant related work in generative modeling
Proposed methodology is not well-supported by experimental setup and results are not sufficiently explained
Diversity is mentioned as main motivation in abstract but lacks theoretical or empirical support
Contributions do not appear significant due to insufficient validation of claims
The core assumption that generated distribution should exactly match training set attribute distribution is not justified
No theoretical guarantees or references provided for the underlying assumptions about feature distribution
suggestions: Provide clearer explanation of how text attribute centers are calculated and used
Better define and explain accuracy metrics in Table 1, including what 'positive samples' means and alignment criteria
Clarify how each channel of the embedding is utilized in Section 3.1
Expand literature survey to cover more related work in generative modeling
Provide more comprehensive experimental validation comparing against existing metrics
Add theoretical justification or empirical evidence for the assumption that generated distributions should match training set distributions exactly
Improve technical soundness of the proposed methodology
Address inconsistencies between text descriptions and figure captions (e.g., Figure 4 caption discrepancy)
Provide references to support claims about model behavior and feature distribution generation
Expand experimental setup to better validate the metric's effectiveness
main_points: The metric's reliance on predefined text attributes significantly limits its applicability and generalizability
The assumption that generated images should have exactly the same attribute distribution as training data is questionable and unjustified
Using embeddings from different models (image encoder vs text encoder) with different downstream training tasks may not be appropriate for similarity calculations
The novelty is limited as the approach primarily combines existing techniques (CLIPScore and KL Divergence)
The paper lacks sufficient experimental validation to support its claims about metric effectiveness
Diversity is presented as a main motivation but lacks adequate theoretical or empirical support
technical_details: The metric uses CLIP image encoder and text encoder which have different embedding spaces influenced by different downstream tasks
Directional CLIPScore is calculated as cosine similarity between direction vectors derived from cluster centers
SaKLD uses KL divergence between probability density functions of DCS values for each attribute
PaKLD extends SaKLD to consider joint probability distributions of attribute pairs
Gaussian kernel density estimation is used to estimate PDFs with 10,000 sampled points
The paper uses N=20 attributes in experiments
Three attribute selection methods are proposed: BLIP extraction, user annotation, and GPT-3 based generation
Table 1 shows DCS has mean accuracy of 0.530 on refined CelebA attributes vs 0.409 for CLIPScore, but the definition of accuracy is unclear
The center of images (CX) and center of text attributes (CT) are computed as mean embeddings from the respective encoders
The paper does not clearly explain how embedding channels are utilized in the methodology

title: Residual Scheduling: A New Reinforcement Learning Approach to Solving Job Shop Scheduling Problem

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a deep reinforcement learning-based constructive heuristic to solve the (Flexible) Job Shop Scheduling Problem. An instance of the problem is represented as a graph and fed into a Graph Neural Network-based model which outputs a score for each candidate (operation-machine) pair. The model is trained with the REINFORCE algorithm with as baseline a classic Priority Dispatching Rule-based heuristic. The novelty of the paper lies in the update of the state after each action: the graph is updated by removing the operations which have already been executed to focus on the most relevant information, which is the residual operations and remaining times of the ongoing ones. The proposed approach is experimentally shown to outperform RL-based constructive heuristics on classic JSSP and FJSSP benchmarks.
reviews.strengths:
1. Sound and interesting idea of removing irrelevant information from the state 
1. The paper is fairly clear and I appreciated the illustrations Fig 1-3.
1. The model was proposed for the JSSP and easily adapted to the Flexible JSSP
1. The approach outperforms deep RL-based construction heuristics on classic benchmarks
reviews.weaknesses:
1. Limited novelty: the main contribution is the definition of the residual state at each step of the construction process by removing irrelevant operations and resetting the time reference. This seems to me an incremental improvement of the approach L2D [1], which already proposed a similar state graph representation and the use of the Graph Isomorphism Network architecture for the JSSP.
1. The proposed approach seems very specific to (F)JSSPs (state representation, baseline) and it's not clear what could be transferable to DRL heuristics for solving other optimization problems.
1. In the experiments, the presented non-learning-based baselines seem pretty weak: only greedy (see more in Questions)
1. Lots of English typos (I noted a few per page)

[1] C Zhang et al, Learning to Dispatch for Job Shop Scheduling via Deep Reinforcement Learning, Neurips 2020
reviews.questions:
1. Where dos the attributes of edges (L205) appear in the GNN model (Sec 3.3)?
1. The paper claims: “Interestingly, using RS, the average gaps are nearly zero for the collections with sizes larger than 100 … A strong implication is that our RS approach can be scaled up for job sizes and even reach the optimal for sufficient large job count.” Do you have an idea of why the model would work better on unseen large instances versus instances of the same size as the training ones? Does OR-tools return the optimal solutions for these larger instances? Can it be that the quality of the reference solutions decreases and therefore the “optimality” gap becomes smaller?
1. Are there stronger non-learning-based baselines for the JSPP other than the greedy PDR heuristics presented in Table 2? To be able to appreciate the performance of the proposed approach, it would be useful and more convincing to compare it to the best heuristics for this problem, beyond simple greedy ones. For example maybe [2]?
1. Appendix, Algorithm 1, Line 6:  to compute this makespan at state s_t, given action a_t, do you do a rollout of the current policy \pi_{\theta} with the updated parameter \theta? This would mean doing a rollout until the end of both the baseline and current policy at each step of the trajectory? How long did the training take?
1. How is the average computation time computed? (Table 13) In particular, was the policy applied to each instance individually or were instances batched? 
1. The discussion L322 about the time it takes for RS/L2D/ScheduleNet versus OR-Tools can be a bit misleading: OR-tools is an exact solver. If spends a lot of time proving the optimality of the solution. Probably it could return a good quality solution much faster if used as a heuristic. In addition it runs on CPUs and not GPUs therefore just comparing the time is not really fair. 

[2] CY Zhang, A tabu search algorithm with a new neighborhood structure for the job shop scheduling problem, Computers & Operations Research, 2007
reviews.limitations: Not explicitly addressed by the paper.
reviews.rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 5: You are absolutely certain about your assessment. You are very familiar with the related work and checked the math/other details carefully.
